rules.description: Named const before every meaningful return. No call expression, ternary, or anonymous object on the return line itself. Exemption: void-terminator form — a function whose last statement is a side-effect call returning void/undefined (console.log, process.stdout.write, etc.) MAY end with that bare statement and an implicit return. Wrapping void calls into `const X = sideEffect(); return X;` is a ceremonial violation (see ceremonial-void-return).
Function and method names must not use banned verbs: handle, do, run, execute, perform. Exception: 'handle' is allowed only for DOM event handlers (e.g., handleClick, handleSubmit).
All boolean variables must carry a semantic prefix: is, has, can, should, did, needs, supports, allows.
No magic strings, template literals, or anonymous objects passed directly as assertion arguments. Extract into named consts first.

No computed expression passed directly as an argument in any function call. Covered forms: (a) function/method calls — includes the high-frequency test idiom `assert.ok(actual.includes(expected))` / `assert.ok(actual.startsWith(expected))` / `assert.ok(actual.match(pattern))` / `assert.ok(obj.error.message.includes(text))` — the method call is the boolean subject and MUST be extracted to `const hasExpected = actual.includes(expected);` (or `hasPrefix`, `hasMatch`, etc.) first; zero tolerance even when the method argument is a plain literal, (b) ternaries, (c) template-literals with interpolation `${...}`, (d) concatenations with `+`, (e) anonymous object literals used as configuration, (f) binary-comparison expressions (`<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `===`, `!==`, `==`, `!=`) passed as boolean subject — e.g. `assert.ok(actualBytes < 2700, ...)` must extract `const isUnderLimit = actualBytes < 2700;` first, (g) unary-negation of a call or property access (`!fn(x)`, `!arr.includes(x)`, `!obj.prop`) passed as boolean subject — e.g. `assert.ok(!actual.includes(pattern), ...)` must extract `const isPatternAbsent = !actual.includes(pattern);` first. Extract into a named const first so the callsite reads as a line of the process. Exempt: plain string literals ('utf8'), plain number literals (2), plain boolean literals, and direct function references (fs.existsSync) — these carry no composition and adding a named const for them is ceremony. Printer-function exemption: when the enclosing function body is exactly one call of `console.*` / `process.stdout.write` / `process.stderr.write` and the function name already carries the intent (`printX`, `logX`, `warnX`), the function name serves as the semantic const — do not extract the template into an extra variable, it would duplicate the function's own naming. Test-framework title exemption: positional template-literal titles in consagrated APIs (`it(\`should X ${const}\`)`, `describe(\`...\`)`) are test-name composition, not process-narrative — do not extract. Scope: all callsites (assert, throw, console.log, fs.*, execSync, and any user call). Violation is at the argument position, not in variable assignment RHS, object property values, or array elements. Extracted boolean consts must honor `boolean-prefix` (is/has/can/should/did/needs/supports/allows).

Variable and parameter names must not be exactly a banned noun (standalone, not as part of a compound name). The full identifier must equal the banned word — compound names like `packageData`, `ruleObj`, `existingItems` are not violations. Banned nouns: data, info, obj, item, thing, res, req, ctx, cb, fn, val, tmp, arr.
A function should either orchestrate (call named functions) or implement (compute/transform) — not both. Functions that mix levels of abstraction violate the Single Level of Abstraction rule.
rules.exampleViolation: function processOrder(order) { validateOrder(order); const total = order.items.reduce((sum, i) => sum + i.price, 0); }
rules.exampleFix: function processOrder(order) { validateOrder(order); const total = computeOrderTotal(order); }

rules.description: Deeply nested conditionals (3+ levels) instead of guard clauses. Prefer early returns to reduce nesting.

Logically distinct groups of statements must be separated by exactly one blank line. Zero blank lines within a group. Never two or more consecutive blank lines. Groups are defined by semantic intent, not by statement type.

Do not wrap lexically-void side-effect primitives into `const X = call(...); return X;`. Flagged call targets: console.log, console.error, console.warn, console.info, process.stdout.write, process.stderr.write, and any method whose name starts with `print` (print*, e.g. printUsage, BundleUI.printHelp). These are known statement-style terminators; the named const holds undefined forever and satisfies explaining-returns mechanically while destroying its intent. Fix: emit the bare side-effect statement and let the function return implicitly (use `return;` only when serving as early-return guard before further branches). Does NOT apply to orchestrators calling named subfunctions via the canonical 2-statement `Pure Entry Point` form — that form is project convention (see code-style.md PreStartGate) and stands even when the subfunction happens to return undefined. Scope: leaf sites where the RHS itself is one of the flagged primitives.
